metadata.title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

messages.say:
what’s the correct mental model between a server, a sidecar, spinning up multiple processes from one deployed machine, services, and multiple servers


bun/node

Start with the clean split: you are mixing three different axes.


One axis is process topology: how many operating system processes are running.
Another is network topology: how many ports/endpoints/servers are accepting requests.
Another is logical architecture: how many “services” exist as separate responsibilities.


Those are related, but not the same thing. Most confusion comes from collapsing them.


The most useful mental model in Bun or Node is this:


A server is usually a process that listens for requests on a port or socket. In Bun/Node terms, that is your Bun.serve(...), http.createServer(...), Fastify app, Elysia app, and so on. If something is actually binding to :3000, that thing is a server.


A process is just a running operating system program. One deployed machine can run one process or twenty. A process may be a server, or it may be a worker, scheduler, cron loop, queue consumer, file watcher, etc. Not every process is a server.


A service is mostly a logical unit of capability. “Auth service,” “billing service,” “email service,” “search service.” Sometimes that maps to one process. Sometimes several services live inside one process. Sometimes one service spans multiple processes. “Service” is an architecture word, not a precise runtime word.


A sidecar is a companion process deployed alongside a main process, usually on the same machine, container group, or pod, and tightly coupled to it operationally. It is not just “another process.” It exists to support the main app: proxying, telemetry, secrets injection, log shipping, service mesh, local queue bridge, file sync, etc.


A machine is the host. One VM, one container host, one bare-metal box, one Railway instance depending on platform semantics. Multiple processes can run on one machine. Multiple servers can run on one machine, each on different ports.


Now the clean mapping.


If you have one Bun app listening on port 3000, that is:


1 machine
1 process
1 server
maybe 1 service, maybe many logical services inside it


If you have an API on port 3000 and a background worker in another Bun process on the same machine, that is:


1 machine
2 processes
1 server + 1 worker
maybe still 1 product/system
possibly 2 logical services, depending on boundaries


If you have API on 3000 and admin panel backend on 3001, both on same machine:


1 machine
2 processes
2 servers
possibly 2 services


If you have API on 3000 and an OpenTelemetry Collector or Envoy proxy next to it:


1 machine
2 processes
1 main app server + 1 sidecar


That sidecar may itself also be a server internally, but the reason it exists is what matters. “Sidecar” describes the deployment relationship, not whether it listens on a port.


Here is the wrong mental model that breaks people:


“Every service is a server and every server is a different machine.”


False.


A single Node/Bun monolith can contain many services logically. One machine can host many processes. One process can expose multiple routes and act as several logical services. A sidecar can be a server, but not every adjacent server is a sidecar.


The better mental model is to think in layers.


At the bottom is the host:


Machine / container / VM
  -> processes
      -> some processes open ports (servers)
      -> some processes do background work


On top of that is architecture:


Product/system
  -> logical services/capabilities
      -> implemented by one or more processes


That is the separation you want.


In Bun/Node specifically, these are the common runtime shapes.


The simplest shape is the single-process app. One Bun process. One server. Routes handle API, maybe even simple scheduled jobs. Good for small systems. Breaks when background work becomes long-running, failure-prone, or resource-heavy.


Next is API + worker. One process serves requests, another consumes jobs. This is usually the first real split that matters. Not because “microservices,” but because request/response latency and background execution have different lifecycles. If image processing or emails or imports can crash or stall, keep them out of the web server process.


Then API + worker + sidecar(s). Example: Bun API, Bun worker, local OpenTelemetry Collector sidecar, maybe Redis or a reverse proxy on the same machine. This is operational composition, not necessarily distributed architecture.


Then multiple independent servers/services. Separate auth server, API server, workflow server, websocket server. They may run on one machine in dev, and different machines in prod. The mental model should survive that transition.


A sidecar deserves extra precision because people misuse it.


A real sidecar usually has these properties:
it is deployed next to one primary app instance, it has a narrow support role, it often shares lifecycle with the app, and the app can often talk to it over localhost or shared files/network namespace.


Examples:
an Nginx reverse proxy in front of your Node app,
an OpenTelemetry Collector receiving traces from your app,
an Envoy proxy,
a local secrets agent,
a file-sync/log-forwarding helper.


A database is usually not called a sidecar. Redis usually is not a sidecar unless you are very specifically running an instance solely to support that app instance locally. More often those are just backing services.


That distinction matters:


A backing service is a dependency your app talks to.
A sidecar is a companion process deployed with your app.


Examples:


Node API -> Postgres


Postgres is a backing service, not a sidecar.


Node API -> localhost:4317 OpenTelemetry Collector


The collector is a sidecar.


Now the subtle part: multiple processes from one deployed machine.


This is completely normal. The machine is just capacity. Processes are isolated runtimes. You can run:


bun api.ts
bun worker.ts
bun scheduler.ts
bun websocket.ts


all on one box.


That does not automatically mean you have “microservices.” You may just have one system with several runtime roles. Whether they are separate services depends on whether they have independent contracts, ownership boundaries, deployment cadence, scaling needs, and failure domains.


That gives you the practical test:


If two pieces must always deploy together, share the same codebase, same database, same ownership, same release timing, and mostly just have different runtime roles, they are probably not separate services. They are separate processes in one service or one system.


If two pieces can be versioned, scaled, deployed, observed, and reasoned about independently, they are closer to separate services.


A useful matrix:


Term       | What it describes
-----------|------------------------------
Machine    | Host/runtime environment
Process    | OS execution unit
Server     | Process that listens for requests
Service    | Logical capability/boundary
Sidecar    | Companion support process deployed next to a main app


Now the Bun/Node-specific traps.


First trap: “Node is single-threaded, so one machine means one thing.”
Wrong. One Node/Bun process has one main event loop, but you can run multiple processes easily. cluster, process managers, containers, separate deploy commands, and platform process formation all exist.


Second trap: “If I spawn another process from my app, that means sidecar.”
Not really. That usually just means child process or worker process. A sidecar is an operational/deployment pattern, not a JavaScript API choice.


Third trap: “If two servers run on different ports, they are different services.”
Not necessarily. They may just be split entrypoints. Port count is not architecture.


Fourth trap: “A service package in code should become a separate deployed server.”
No. Code packaging and deployment packaging do not need 1:1 alignment. You can keep strong service boundaries in code and still deploy as one server/process until scale or isolation demands a split.


Here is the sanity check I would use.


Ask five questions:


1. Does it listen on its own port/socket?
   -> then it is a server.

2. Is it a separate OS runtime?
   -> then it is a separate process.

3. Is it mainly there to support another process locally?
   -> then it is probably a sidecar.

4. Is it a distinct business/system capability?
   -> then it may be a service.

5. Can it be deployed and scaled independently without lying to yourself?
   -> then it is more truly its own service/server unit.


Concrete examples.


Example 1: Bun API plus queue consumer.


machine
  - process A: Bun API server on :3000
  - process B: Bun worker consumes jobs from Redis


Correct model: one machine, two processes, one server plus one worker, maybe one product with two runtime roles. Not necessarily two services.


Example 2: Bun API plus OpenTelemetry Collector.


machine
  - process A: Bun API server on :3000
  - process B: otel collector on :4317


Correct model: API server plus sidecar.


Example 3: Auth server and application server on same box.


machine
  - process A: auth server on :3001
  - process B: app server on :3000


Correct model: two servers, two processes, maybe two services, same machine.


Example 4: One big Elysia app with /auth, /billing, /search.


Correct model: one server, one process, multiple logical services/modules inside one deployable app.


That is usually the right default for small and mid-sized Bun/Node systems: keep logical boundaries strong and runtime/deployment boundaries weak until needed.


So the mental model I would actually carry forward is this:


Build in code as if services are logical boundaries.
Deploy initially as few processes as possible.
Split into separate processes when runtime behavior differs.
Split into separate servers when network/API isolation is needed.
Call something a sidecar only when it is an operational companion to a main app instance.


The practical default stack for most Bun/Node projects is:


Start:
  1 process, 1 server

Then:
  1 server process + 1 worker process

Then maybe:
  + observability/proxy sidecar

Only later:
  multiple independently deployed servers/services


That path preserves simplicity without lying about architecture.


The baseline sanity check is this: if you say “service” and cannot answer whether you mean code boundary, process boundary, or deployment boundary, your model is too fuzzy and will cause bad architecture decisions.


The fix is to always name the layer explicitly:
“service module,” “worker process,” “API server,” “sidecar collector,” “shared host.”


That alone removes most of the confusion.


If you want, I’ll map this directly onto your RAWR/plugin/service/host model and show which pieces should stay logical-only versus become separate processes.
Why is a bun worker not a sidecar?

Because “worker” and “sidecar” describe different things.


A worker describes a runtime role: it does background work instead of serving normal request/response traffic.


A sidecar describes a deployment relationship: it is a companion process attached to a main app instance to support it operationally.


Those can overlap, but they are not the same category.


A Bun worker is usually just another application process you wrote. It runs your business logic: send emails, process jobs, execute workflows, consume queue events, run imports, retry failed tasks. That makes it a worker.


A sidecar usually exists to support the main app rather than to embody core product/business logic. Think OpenTelemetry Collector, Envoy proxy, Nginx reverse proxy, secrets agent, log shipper. Those are infrastructure-adjacent helpers.


So the real distinction is not “does it run next to another process?” It is “what is its job in the system?”


Here is the clean test.


Your Bun worker is not a sidecar when:


it contains core application logic

it could reasonably be scaled independently based on workload

it may consume from shared queues across many app instances

it is part of the product/system itself, not just a helper for one local app instance


Example:


api server -> enqueues "generate report"
worker -> generates report, writes DB row, emails user


That worker is part of the application. It is not just helping the API server exist. It is doing real product work.


A process is more like a sidecar when:


it is tightly paired to one app instance

it mainly exists to augment that instance

it is usually not where your business logic lives

if you removed the main app, the sidecar would no longer have a reason to exist in that form


Example:


bun api -> sends traces to localhost collector
otel collector -> batches/exports traces


The collector is a sidecar.


The confusion happens because both may run on the same machine, and both may be “adjacent.” But adjacency is not enough.


This is the deeper mental model:


Worker   = what kind of work the process does
Sidecar  = why that process is colocated with another one


So a Bun worker can be deployed beside an API and still not be a sidecar. It is just a sibling process with a different role.


There is one edge case: a worker can be deployed as a sidecar pattern if it is strictly local to a single app instance and exists only to support that instance. For example, a local file-processing helper that only serves one API instance through local IPC (inter-process communication). Then you could describe it as both:


functionally: a worker

topologically: a sidecar


But that is not the usual meaning when people say “background worker” in Node/Bun systems.


Sanity check:


If the process disappeared, would the system lose a core product capability?
- yes -> probably an app worker/service process
- no, mostly observability/networking/support -> probably a sidecar


That is the practical distinction.


In your architecture conversations, calling a Bun worker a sidecar too early will break your thinking because it makes core application runtimes sound like optional support helpers. They are not. A worker is usually part of the application topology, not a sidecar to it.

Okay so in RAWR, if we expand our full set of plugins to include cron/background/worker, in addition to API plugins and workflow plugins and CLI plugins, given the hosting model—how would you categorize each?

Thought for 6s


Thought for 17s


Called tool


Thought for 22s


Called tool

Called tool


The cleanest categorization is: do not treat these as equivalent “plugin kinds.” They live on different axes.


In this repo, the target model is already explicit: shared capability logic lives in packages/*, runtime adapters live in plugins/*, final assembly lives in rawr.hq.ts, and hosts in apps/* mount those surfaces rather than inventing their own capability wiring


PLUGINS


[


SYSTEM


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/SYSTEM.md). The current manifest already shows the shape: one composed oRPC router, one workflow trigger router, and one host-owned Inngest bundle per process[


rawr.hq


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/rawr.hq.ts).


So the right mental model is not “plugin type = deployment type.” It is:


packages/* = capability/kernel/domain

plugins/* = surface adapters

apps/* = actual hosts/processes

rawr.hq.ts = composition authority


From that, here is how I would categorize each.


API plugins


These are boundary-surface adapters for the shared server host. They are not workers, not sidecars, and not standalone services by default. They adapt package capabilities into caller-facing oRPC/OpenAPI routes. That is already the documented target: plugins/api/* own boundary contracts/routers, and the server mounts them into /rpc and /api/orpc/* via manifest composition


PLUGINS


[


ARCHITECTURE


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/projects/orpc-ingest-workflows-spec/ARCHITECTURE.md)[


rawr


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/apps/server/src/rawr.ts).


So category:


logical category: boundary adapter

runtime category: server-mounted plugin

deployment category: usually in the main server process

process model: not a sidecar, not a worker


Workflow plugins


These are dual-surface plugins. This is the one people blur.


A workflow plugin has:


a caller-facing trigger surface mounted under /api/workflows/<capability>/*

a durable execution surface implemented as Inngest functions behind /api/inngest


That split is explicitly locked in the spec: API boundary and durable execution remain split; workflow triggers are caller-facing; /api/inngest is runtime ingress only; one runtime-owned Inngest bundle exists per process


ARCHITECTURE


. The manifest reflects that by exporting both workflows.triggerRouter and inngest.{ client, functions, handler }[


rawr.hq


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/rawr.hq.ts), and the server host mounts them in that order[


rawr


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/apps/server/src/rawr.ts).


So category:


logical category: durable-workflow adapter

runtime category: split between boundary trigger surface and workflow runtime surface

deployment category: usually shared with the server host at first, but conceptually the durable half is closer to a worker runtime

process model: not a sidecar; it is part server adapter, part durable worker topology


This is the key distinction: a workflow plugin is not “just another API plugin.” It straddles triggering and execution.


CLI plugins


These are local command-surface adapters. They are not server plugins at all. They adapt package capabilities into command invocations through the rawr entrypoint. The docs already call out plugins/cli/* as a plugin root and rawr.hq.ts as the place where CLI command registries are composed for host mounting


SYSTEM


.


So category:


logical category: operator/developer command adapter

runtime category: local process entry surface

deployment category: runs in the CLI host process when invoked

process model: ephemeral foreground process, not a server, not a sidecar, not a worker by default


Cron plugins


I would not make cron a peer semantic category beside API, workflows, and CLI.


Cron is not really a surface. It is a trigger mode.


If the cron job exists to kick off durable execution, it belongs under workflow plugins. The schedule is just another ingress into the workflow runtime. Inngest already naturally fits this shape. So “cron workflow” is a workflow plugin with a schedule trigger, not a new top-level plugin kind.


Only make a distinct cron plugin kind if you are saying, very specifically, “this thing is host-scheduled and not part of the durable workflow system.” That is a weaker, less coherent model.


So my recommendation:


do not create plugins/cron/* as a primary kind

treat cron as either:
a workflow trigger mode, or

a host scheduler registration attached to a worker/background runtime


Background plugins


This is too vague to be a good primary kind.


“Background” describes execution posture, not interface shape. It tells you that something should not block a request, but it does not tell you whether the thing is:


scheduled,

queue-driven,

event-driven,

durable,

ephemeral,

in-process,

isolated in another process.


So I would avoid background as a canonical plugin root. It is a smell category, like naming something utils.


If you keep it at all, it should be a second-order classification inside a more concrete kind:


workflow plugin with background execution

worker plugin with background consumption

API plugin that enqueues background work


Worker plugins


This one can be legitimate, but only if you define it sharply.


A worker plugin should mean: a plugin whose primary role is runtime execution/consumption, not boundary exposure. That could mean queue consumers, stream consumers, daemon loops, or continuously running processors. Unlike workflow plugins, which are specifically durable-execution adapters around an Inngest-style model, worker plugins would cover non-Inngest long-running runtime roles.


So category:


logical category: execution/runtime adapter

runtime category: non-boundary process role

deployment category: usually separate worker process, though could share host initially

process model: sibling application process, not sidecar by default


This is where your earlier sidecar question matters. A worker plugin is usually not a sidecar because it carries core application behavior, can scale independently, and is part of the product/system runtime rather than merely supporting another app instance.


The real categorization stack I would use in RAWR is this:


Primary plugin kinds by surface


api = caller-facing boundary adapter

workflows = durable trigger + execution adapter

cli = command adapter

web = frontend/web runtime adapter

agents = agent capability adapter

mcp = MCP action adapter


That matches the current docs exactly


PLUGINS


[


SYSTEM


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/SYSTEM.md).


Then add a separate runtime-role axis:


Runtime roles


server-mounted

worker-executed

scheduled

ephemeral-cli

browser/web

agent-runtime


That is the missing clarity.


So if you expand the system, I would categorize your proposed additions like this:


API plugin: surface kind

Workflow plugin: surface kind with durable runtime

CLI plugin: surface kind

Cron: trigger mode, not primary kind

Background: execution posture, not primary kind

Worker: runtime role or possibly a distinct kind only for non-workflow execution plugins


That means the good model is not:


api | workflows | cli | cron | background | worker


That is mixing surface kinds with runtime roles and trigger styles.


The better model is:


Surface kinds:
api | workflows | cli | web | agents | mcp

Runtime roles / execution modes:
server-mounted | worker | scheduled | background | interactive


Then the combinations become obvious:


API plugin → server-mounted

Workflow plugin → server-mounted trigger surface + worker/durable execution runtime

CLI plugin → interactive/ephemeral

Scheduled workflow → workflow plugin + scheduled trigger

Queue consumer → worker plugin or workflow plugin, depending on whether it is Inngest-native durable execution

Background email sender → usually workflow plugin or worker runtime, not “background plugin”


Sanity check against the repo’s current host model:


The server host today mounts /api/inngest, /api/workflows/*, and /rpc + /api/orpc/* in explicit order


rawr


. The manifest is already the single authority that composes oRPC routes and Inngest runtime seams[


rawr.hq


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/rawr.hq.ts). That means your architecture already wants surface-first classification with host-owned runtime composition. Lean into that.


My blunt recommendation:


Do not add background as a top-level plugin type.
Do not add cron as a top-level plugin type.
Only add worker as a top-level plugin type if you need a real non-Inngest execution surface.


Otherwise:


cron belongs to workflows

background belongs to runtime semantics

worker is either workflows or a separate execution host class


The strongest version of the model is:


plugins/* should classify how the system is extended at an interface/runtime seam, not every incidental way code happens to run.


If you want, I can turn this into a concrete proposed directory + manifest contract for plugins/workers/* versus folding scheduled/background execution into plugins/workflows/*.

Lets say we expect to grow a little bit. Right now everything runs on one host. However, I can imagine that the host for the server vs. the async execution runs sent by inngest vs. background worker plugins may want their own processes or machines in the future. I don’t know.


What would the minimal yet performant architecture for this look like? Or is it really all fine running on “one host”? Should we keep one “host” mental model such that 1 plugin system : 1 host and the plugin types don’t matter? Or does that break?

The right answer is: keep one HQ/plugin system, but do not collapse that into “there is one host forever, so plugin types don’t matter.”


That breaks as soon as runtime behavior diverges.


The clean model is:


1 HQ/plugin system
N host roles


Not:


1 plugin system = 1 physical host/process forever


Your repo is already pointing in that direction. rawr.hq.ts is the single composition authority for surfaces and runtime seams, while host apps mount those exports instead of re-authoring capability wiring


rawr.hq


[


SYSTEM


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/SYSTEM.md). The workflow architecture also explicitly separates boundary APIs from durable execution and says there is one runtime-owned Inngest bundle per process, not one globally forever[


ARCHITECTURE


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/projects/orpc-ingest-workflows-spec/ARCHITECTURE.md). That “per process” wording matters.


So the mental model that scales is:


one logical HQ

one plugin registry/composition authority

potentially several runtime hosts/process roles


Those roles can all run on one machine today and separate later without changing plugin semantics.


The minimal yet performant architecture is this:


Stage 1: one machine, two or three processes max


Keep everything on one box if the load is modest. That is fine. What matters is separating runtime roles, not necessarily physical machines.


The default shape I would use is:


Machine A
  - process 1: server host
      mounts:
      - /rpc
      - /api/orpc/*
      - /api/workflows/<capability>/*
      - /api/inngest (if needed initially)

  - process 2: async runtime host
      runs:
      - Inngest durable execution
      - background worker plugins
      - scheduled jobs / cron triggers

  - optional process 3: web host / asset host
      only if needed separately


This is minimal, because it is still one machine.
This is performant, because you isolate request/response latency from async execution.
This is scalable, because you can move process 2 to another machine later with minimal conceptual churn.


That is the architecture I would recommend.


Why not keep literally everything in one process?


Because the first real split in systems like this is not “microservices.” It is foreground vs background.


Your server host has different requirements:


low latency

stable memory

predictable tail latency

request-scoped auth/context

easy horizontal scaling later


Your async execution host has different requirements:


retries

long-running work

burst tolerance

looser latency requirements

separate concurrency controls

safe failure isolation


If you keep them in one process too long, this will break because:


a burst of async work will steal CPU and memory from user-facing requests

one bad workflow/plugin can destabilize the main server

concurrency tuning becomes impossible without compromising one side

observability gets muddier because foreground and background work share one pool and one failure domain


So “one host” is fine as a machine model for now.
It is not fine as a single runtime role model once you expect growth.


That means the best mental model is:


one system, one manifest, multiple host roles


I would define the host roles like this.


1. Server host


This is the caller-facing boundary host.


It mounts:


API plugins

workflow trigger routers

maybe web plugins if server-served

maybe CLI is separate, not here


Its job is:


accept requests

authenticate/authorize

validate contracts

enqueue or trigger async work

return responses quickly


In your current architecture, this corresponds to the mounted route families in apps/server/src/rawr.ts: /api/inngest, /api/workflows/*, and /rpc + /api/orpc/* in explicit order


rawr


. But for future shape, I would treat /api/inngest as the thing most likely to move off this host first in responsibility, even if the HTTP ingress point remains nearby.


2. Async execution host


This is the execution/runtime host.


It runs:


Inngest durable functions

worker plugins

scheduled triggers

queue consumers

long-running/background logic


Its job is not to be the public server.
Its job is to execute work reliably.


This host is where I would put:


workflow durable execution

worker plugins

cron/scheduled plugins


That gives you one clean runtime class for “non-request work.”


3. CLI host


This is already separate by nature. CLI plugins are not part of the long-running server topology. They are invoked processes. Treat them as their own host class conceptually, even if they run on the same machine during development.


Now the plugin categorization becomes much cleaner.


Do not think “plugin types don’t matter.”
They do matter, but not because each one deserves its own machine.


They matter because each plugin type attaches to one or more host roles.


A better mapping looks like this:


API plugins
  -> server host

Workflow plugins
  -> server host (trigger surface)
  -> async execution host (durable execution)

Worker plugins
  -> async execution host

Cron/scheduled behavior
  -> async execution host
  -> usually as a trigger mode, not a whole new top-level worldview

CLI plugins
  -> CLI host

Web plugins
  -> web host and/or server host depending on delivery model


That is the model that survives growth.


Now the practical question: is one machine still okay?


Yes, absolutely, if you split by process role.


A very good near-term setup is:


Railway service A: server
Railway service B: async
Optional Railway service C: web
Shared Postgres / Redis / backing services


Or if you truly want one deployed machine:


one VM/container host
  supervises:
    bun run server
    bun run async


That is enough for a long time.


The thing I would not do is make plugin type the unit of deployment. That becomes nonsense fast.


You do not want:


one process for each API plugin

one process for each workflow plugin

one process for each worker plugin


That is fake modularity and real ops pain.


The deployment unit should be host role, not plugin kind.


So the actual answer to your “1 plugin system : 1 host” question is:


Keep 1 plugin system : 1 composition authority.
Do not keep 1 plugin system : 1 runtime host forever.


That breaks once execution modes differ.


The best durable mental model is:


One HQ/plugin system
  -> one manifest/composition authority
  -> many plugin surfaces
  -> mounted into a small number of host roles
  -> each host role can run as one process today and separate later


That gives you minimal complexity now and a clean migration path later.


Here is the staged path I would recommend.


Stage A: now


one machine

one server process

one async process

same repo, same plugin system, same manifest authority


Stage B: moderate growth


separate deploy units for server and async

same plugin contracts

same rawr.hq composition semantics

host-specific mounting/build steps


Stage C: only if needed


isolate especially hot or risky worker classes

maybe dedicated workflow runner or heavy compute worker

maybe separate web serving

still do not split by plugin kind blindly


Here is the sanity check.


If a runtime role has different needs in:


latency,

concurrency,

failure tolerance,

scaling,

uptime,

or resource profile,


then it should probably be its own host process.


By that test:


server and async should be separate soon

CLI is already separate by nature

web may or may not be separate

individual plugin kinds usually should not each get their own process


My blunt recommendation:


Do not keep the mental model that plugin types do not matter.
Do not keep the mental model that one HQ equals one forever-host.


Keep:


one HQ

one manifest authority

two primary long-running host roles: server and async

optional web and CLI host roles as needed


That is the minimal architecture that is still honest.


The baseline version in concrete terms:


HQ / plugin system
  rawr.hq.ts = composition authority

Host roles
  server-host:
    mounts api + workflow triggers

  async-host:
    runs inngest durable execution + worker plugins + schedules

reverse proxy

service mesh proxy

OpenTelemetry (OpenTelemetry) collector

log shipper

secrets agent


Those are infrastructure-support roles.


Your async host is not that. It carries core product execution:


Inngest durable workflows

schedules

consumers

background business jobs

maybe private internal service routers


That is not support glue. That is part of the application itself.


So the clean distinction is:


server = caller-facing runtime role
async  = background execution runtime role


Both are first-class.


A sidecar would be more like:


server process
  + otel collector sidecar
  + proxy sidecar


or


async process
  + queue bridge sidecar
  + telemetry sidecar


The async host itself is not the sidecar. It is one of the main runtime lanes of the system.


The sanity check is simple:


If removing the process removes a core business capability, it is probably not a sidecar.


If you remove your async host, you lose:


workflow execution

retries

scheduled jobs

background processing


That is not optional support. That is a core runtime.


So do not think:


server = main
async = sidecar


Think:


RAWR host bundle
  -> server role
  -> async role


Both are peers under the same host bundle.


Now, there is one narrow case where something async-ish could be sidecar-like:
if you had a tiny colocated helper process that only existed to support a single server instance locally and was not a real shared application runtime. Example:


a local queue pump

a local cache warmer

a local bridge daemon


That could be described as a sidecar.


But that is not what you are building. Your async host is the durable execution plane for the system. That makes it a first-class host role, not a sidecar.


So the correct mental model is:


server and async are sibling runtime roles

both can run on one machine or separate machines

neither is inherently a sidecar of the other

actual sidecars are optional supporting infrastructure attached to either one

Yes. It is still async from the caller’s point of view, but only if the API is not waiting for the actual business work to finish.


The key distinction is not “did it use RPC immediately?” The key distinction is “what does the callee do before returning?”


There are two very different cases.


Case 1:


API -> internal RPC -> do the real work now -> return result


That is not really async in the important architectural sense. It is just a synchronous hop behind another boundary. The client returned only after the work finished somewhere else.


Case 2:


API -> internal RPC -> enqueue/trigger durable work -> return ack/runId


That is async. The API fires immediately, the internal boundary accepts the request, hands it to the async execution system, and returns before the real work is done.


So “immediately and returns” by itself does not answer the question. What matters is whether that internal API is:


executing the work inline, or

just triggering execution elsewhere


If your API calls an internal RPC route whose whole job is:


validate trusted input

trigger an Inngest function or emit an event

return { accepted: true, runId }


then yes, that is absolutely async, even though the trigger call itself is immediate.


That is actually the normal shape.


Think of it like this:


sync transport != sync work


You can use a synchronous transport call to initiate asynchronous execution.


That is probably the cleanest way to think about your model.


So in RAWR terms, a server-side API plugin could call an internal private router over an oRPC link, and that internal router could do one of two things:


Bad async model:


server API
  -> internal router
  -> heavy business logic executes here
  -> return


This is not really async. You just moved the synchronous work behind a private boundary.


Good async model:


server API
  -> internal router
  -> trigger workflow / enqueue job
  -> return ack
async host
  -> picks up execution
  -> does heavy work


This is async.


So the internal RPC link is not the thing that determines sync vs async. It is just a transport boundary.


The actual determinant is:


Does this boundary perform work now, or schedule work now?


That is the sanity check.


If you want the clean architecture, your “internal” API on the async side should usually be one of these:


A trigger surface:
it accepts a request and starts durable execution.


An internal query/control surface:
health, status, cancellation, admin control, maybe workflow state inspection.


What it should usually not be is “the place where long-running business logic executes inline over RPC,” unless you very intentionally want that.


Because then you have not created an async plane. You have created a second synchronous service.


So the clean version is:


synchronous RPC to a trigger endpoint is fine

that endpoint should hand off to durable/background execution

returning quickly is what makes the whole thing async to the caller


Formula:


caller latency ≈ trigger latency
not
caller latency ≈ total work latency


If that equation holds, you have real async behavior.


If instead:


caller latency ≈ total work latency


then it is not really async, even if there are two hosts and an internal RPC call in the middle.


So yes, your API can “fire into” an internal API via RPC link and return immediately, and that can still be a correct async model. But only if the internal API is a trigger/dispatch boundary, not the actual execution boundary.

Durable Endpoints are a good fit for some of this, but not as the universal wrapper around every private oRPC internal client.


The core distinction is this:


If you wrap a private service call in a Durable Endpoint and the caller still waits for the final business result, that is still basically a request/response durable API, not your general async plane. Durable Endpoints keep the request/response mental model and are designed to make endpoints fault-tolerant with step retries and observability. On the success path they behave like normal endpoints; on retry paths they can redirect the caller to an Inngest Cloud URL to await the final result. They are currently in beta, support TypeScript and Go, and have limitations including no flow control features like concurrency/rate limiting, no SSE, and currently limited POST body support in the docs. Inngest+3Inngest+3Inngest+3


So your question splits into two cases.


If what you want is:


API -> private internal call -> durable, retryable, observable endpoint -> return final result


then yes, Durable Endpoints can be a very nice fit. That is especially true for “semi-sync” operations where the user still expects a direct result, but you want step checkpointing and recovery. Think complex submit handlers, enrichment pipelines, or multi-step writes that should resume from the point of failure instead of restarting from scratch. Inngest+1


But if what you want is your real async plane:


API -> trigger -> return ack/runId quickly
async runtime -> execute later / separately


then Durable Endpoints are not the cleanest primary primitive. In that model, standard Inngest functions/workflows are a better fit, because the architectural point is not “make this endpoint durable while preserving request/response,” it is “decouple caller latency from work latency.” Durable Endpoints are explicitly about durable APIs that still behave like endpoints. Inngest+1


So your line:

otherwise it’s not async right?

Not quite. “Async” is not determined by whether you used Durable Endpoints. It is determined by whether the caller waits for the work.


Use this sanity check:


caller_latency ≈ total_work_latency
=> not really async, just durable/synchronous

caller_latency ≈ trigger_latency
=> async


That is the real test.


So the architecture I would lock is:


For public or internal request/response flows that should still feel like APIs but need checkpointing, retries, and observability, Durable Endpoints are strong.


For background execution, scheduled jobs, fan-out workflows, and anything where the caller should get back quickly while work continues elsewhere, use normal Inngest workflow/function execution.


That gives you a clean split:


server / API plane
  - normal APIs
  - durable endpoints for “still-an-endpoint” flows

async execution plane
  - Inngest functions/workflows
  - schedules
  - consumers
  - long-running/background work


Now to the naming question.


No, I do not think async is the wrong split name.


It is still the right umbrella because it describes the runtime role, not just one transport pattern. Your async host can contain:


true background workflows,

scheduled execution,

consumers/bridges,

durable endpoint handlers that are operationally part of the async plane,

private internal routers that only trigger work.


“Async” remains a good top-level name because it contrasts with your caller-facing server plane and captures “non-request-lifecycle execution concerns.” It does not require that every single thing in it be fire-and-forget. Some of it can be trigger-and-wait, or durable request/response. The umbrella still fits.


What I would not do is rename the whole thing around Inngest-specific terminology too early. async is a stable semantic. workflow or durable would be narrower and would break the moment you add consumers, internal routers, or non-Inngest helpers.


So the practical recommendation is:


Use Durable Endpoints selectively, not as the universal wrapper for all private service routers.


A good policy would be:


If the caller wants a direct final response and the flow is multi-step/failure-prone, consider Durable Endpoints.

If the caller should get an ack and the work should continue independently, trigger a normal Inngest workflow/function.

Keep async as the host/runtime umbrella name.


My blunt take: wrapping every private oRPC service in Durable Endpoints would blur your architecture. You would start treating “everything nontrivial” like an endpoint, when some of it is actually orchestration and should be modeled as execution, not request/response.


The one uncertainty to keep on the ledger: Durable Endpoints are still beta and the current docs call out meaningful limitations, so I would not make them the sole foundation of your internal runtime model yet. Inngest+2Inngest+2

You are mixing three distinct things:


A boundary plane
A durable/background execution plane
A service call plane


Once those are separated, the whole thing gets simpler.


The clean model for your system is:


server/API plane = caller-facing synchronous boundaries
async plane = durable/background execution
service plane = private callable capability boundaries used by either one


That means the answer to “should we have a synchronous host too?” is:


You already do. That is the server/API host.


You do not need a separate top-level “synchronous host” beyond that unless you are imagining a separate internal service mesh of request/response services. That is usually overkill early, and it is not the right first split for what you are building.


So for your architecture:


public/request-response/synchronous work lives in the server/API plane

durable/background/scheduled work lives in the async plane

private service boundaries can be called from either plane, in-process or over RPC later


Now the missing piece is: what is “long-running or background work” actually?


It is just work where the caller should not sit around waiting for completion, or work that needs its own execution lifecycle.


Typical examples:


send 10,000 emails

import a CSV and normalize records

generate embeddings for a knowledge base

run a billing reconciliation job

fan out notifications

poll an external provider every 5 minutes

consume Stripe or GitHub events and process them robustly

schedule a nightly digest

run a long AI pipeline

retry failed external syncs


Those all have one thing in common: they should not live inline inside a request/response path.


The next confusion is “how does that work begin?”


There are only a few real start modes.


A request triggers it.


User/API request
  -> server route
  -> trigger background work
  -> return ack/runId


A schedule triggers it.


Cron/scheduler
  -> trigger background work


An event/consumer triggers it.


Webhook / queue / stream / external event
  -> consumer/bridge
  -> trigger background work


Or, more rarely, a daemon loop runs continuously.


Long-lived process starts
  -> loop / poll / watch / consume forever


That is it. Almost all background work starts one of those four ways.


So when you ask:

Is it just defined once and then called to run?

Yes, mostly.


The normal shape is:


Define the work unit

Define how it gets triggered

Register it with the async host

Something triggers it later


That is the actual mental model.


A concrete example.


Suppose you have a “sales-digest” workflow.


The pieces are:


services/sales/
  logic for building digest content

plugins/async/workflows/sales-digest/
  durable execution definition

plugins/server/api/sales/
  route that triggers digest generation

async host
  mounts workflow bundle and runs it


Flow:


POST /sales/digest
  -> validate request
  -> trigger sales-digest workflow
  -> return { accepted: true, runId }

async host later/now
  -> run sales-digest workflow
  -> call services/sales logic
  -> write DB state
  -> send emails


That is how background work “actually begins.”


Now the other category: long-running background plugins that are not just “trigger one workflow.”


These are more like consumers or daemons.


Examples:


a Stripe event consumer

a GitHub webhook bridge

a Redis/queue consumer

a polling loop for some upstream API

a file watcher

a websocket relay process


Those are not “define once and wait for someone to call a function” in the same way. They are processes that start when the async host starts.


Their lifecycle is:


async host boots
  -> register consumer
  -> consumer subscribes / polls / listens
  -> incoming event arrives
  -> consumer handles directly or triggers a workflow


That is a different shape from an on-demand workflow.


So your async host usually contains two broad categories:


Triggered work:


workflows

scheduled jobs

one-off tasks


Resident runtime adapters:


consumers

bridges

pollers

daemon-like processes


That is why “async host” is a better name than “workflow host.”
It can own both.


Now to your “would it make sense to have a synchronous host as well?” question.


If by “synchronous host” you mean:


“A separate internal host where private request/response service routers live, distinct from the API host”


then the answer is: maybe later, but probably not as a first-class host role now.


Because the normal progression is:


Stage 1:


server/API host handles request/response boundaries

async host handles durable/background execution

private service boundaries are in-process by default


Stage 2:


some private service boundaries move remote

server or async calls them over internal RPC


Stage 3:


if a bounded domain becomes large enough, it gets its own host bundle


So yes, synchronous internal request/response services can exist.
But they are usually not a separate “plane” you need to enshrine from day one.


The two top-level planes you actually need are:


boundary/API plane

execution/async plane


Private request/response service calls are an internal implementation detail across those, not a top-level architecture pillar yet.


That is the part you were overcomplicating.


Now, how would a “long-running or background work plugin” actually look?


There are three useful shapes.


First: workflow plugin


Best when the work is a discrete run with retries/status/steps.


trigger -> workflow run -> done


Use for:


imports

fanout jobs

report generation

AI pipelines

nightly jobs

reconciliation


Second: schedule plugin


Best when the work starts on a clock.


every day at 9am -> trigger workflow


Usually this should trigger a workflow, not contain all business logic inline.


Third: consumer/daemon plugin


Best when the work is driven by an external system or continuous loop.


host start -> subscribe/poll/listen -> event arrives -> handle or trigger workflow


Use for:


queue consumers

provider event bridges

polling loops

watchers


That gives you a nice async subtree:


plugins/
  async/
    workflows/
    schedules/
    consumers/
    internal/


And the execution semantics are clear.


Here is the practical sanity check for where something belongs.


Put it in the API/server plane if:


the caller needs the final answer now

latency matters directly to the caller

it is request validation, auth, or orchestration

the work is short and safe inline


Put it in the async plane if:


caller should get an ack, not final completion

retries matter

it might run for seconds/minutes/hours

it fans out

it depends on flaky external systems

it is scheduled

it is event-driven

it needs isolated concurrency/failure handling


Put it in a consumer/daemon lane if:


it starts when the host starts and keeps running

it listens, polls, or subscribes continuously


That is the whole split.


The biggest misconception to kill is this:


“Background work is some mysterious thing that just runs on its own.”


No. It always has a lifecycle.
It either:


gets triggered,

gets scheduled,

gets consumed from an external source,

or starts as a resident loop.


That’s it.


So the architecture I would lock for you is:


Host roles
  server
    public API routes
    workflow trigger routes
    immediate request/response work

  async
    workflows
    schedules
    consumers/bridges
    private internal routers if needed


And do not add a separate “sync host” right now.
Your server/API host already is that.
A distinct internal synchronous host only becomes real when you have enough remote internal request/response traffic to justify it.


The baseline sanity check:


If the caller waits for final business completion, it belongs in the request/response path.
If the caller only initiates work, it belongs in the async path.
If the process wakes up and listens on its own, it is a consumer/daemon.


That is the clean mental model you were missing.

Yes. That is the idea.


“Called from either plane, in-process or over RPC later” means the service boundary is transport-flexible. It does not mean “all service calls magically belong to the server plane.”


The clean mental model is:


A service boundary is a callable capability boundary.
A host/plane is a runtime location where some service boundaries may be mounted.


So the real question is not “is it RPC?”
The real question is “which host mounted that service boundary?”


That determines where it runs.


There are three cases.


First, in-process.


server host
  -> calls service client
  -> service implementation runs in same process


or


async host
  -> calls service client
  -> service implementation runs in same process


In this mode, there is no network hop. The client is just an in-memory/router client wrapper. This is the simplest and probably your default.


Second, RPC to server host.


async host
  -> RPC link
  -> private service router mounted on server host
  -> service runs on server host


This is valid, but you should only do it if that service truly belongs on the server host.


Third, RPC to async host.


server host
  -> RPC link
  -> private service router mounted on async host
  -> service runs on async host


Also valid, if that service is owned by the async host.


So yes: if something is called over RPC, it runs wherever the target router is mounted. RPC is just transport.


That is the whole answer.


Now to your “that seems weird no?” point.


It only seems weird if you think “server plane” means “public synchronous HTTP only.” But really the server host can have:


public boundary routers

internal trusted routers

in-process services


Likewise the async host can have:


Inngest handlers

consumers/schedulers

internal trusted routers

in-process services


So an “internal server” is not weird. It is just a host exposing trusted-only RPC surfaces on a private network or private port.


The more important rule is: do not mount private services remotely unless there is a reason.

The services/* layer defines:


the service boundary

the canonical router/contract

the transport-neutral client interface


Then a given host decides one of two things:


mount it locally and use it in-process

mount it remotely and call it over RPC


That means the top-level service directory is where the semantic job is done. Host placement is a later deployment/runtime decision.


So the pattern should look like this:

    contract.ts

apps/async/

  target: "server" | "async",
});


The caller should not care much beyond that.


This is why I kept saying “private service boundaries can be called from either plane.” It means:


the server host can call a service

the async host can call a service

the service may be local or remote

transport is an implementation detail behind the client


Now the important architectural guardrail:


Do not turn every service into a remote RPC hop just because you can.


Default should be:


in-process when colocated

RPC only when the service is actually mounted elsewhere


Otherwise you create fake distributed architecture.


A clean rule:


same host/process -> in-process client
different host/process -> RPC client

Now to the subtle part: should services “belong” to server or async?


Not inherently.


A service boundary belongs to a capability, not to a plane.
A plane/host simply mounts the service where it makes operational sense.


Examples:


todo query service might live on server because it serves request/response paths.

billing reconciliation service might live on async because it supports workflows and background jobs.

shared account service might be mounted in both hosts in-process if both need it locally.


That is why the service layer should stay top-level and transport-neutral.


So the final clean model is:


services/* = capability-owned private callable boundaries

server plane = public APIs + whatever services it mounts
async plane  = workflows/consumers + whatever services it mounts

callers use:
  in-process client when local
  RPC link when remote


That is not weird. That is exactly the point of separating service boundaries from host placement.


The sanity check:


If you ask “where does this run?”, answer:
“on whichever host mounted the router.”


If you ask “how do I call it?”, answer:
“through the same service client interface, using in-process or RPC transport depending on placement.”


That is the model to lock.

The question is not “is this architecture seen in the wild?”
It is “what does this extra host buy us relative to what it costs?”


For your context, a dedicated internal services host only makes sense if it is doing real work as a distinct bounded runtime, such as:


owning a genuinely shared internal domain used by multiple hosts

scaling independently for CPU/memory reasons

isolating risky/failure-prone synchronous work from the public API host

becoming its own ecosystem with its own deploy cadence

public API route
  -> internal RPC call
  -> service host
  -> do business logic
  -> return


for almost everything, then your public API host has become a glorified edge adapter. That is usually not a win early.


Why not?


Because you add cost in six places immediately.


First, latency. Every synchronous request now has an extra network hop.


Second, failure modes. A healthy public API host can now fail user requests because the internal services host is degraded.


Third, ops surface. More Railway services, more env vars, more private DNS dependencies, more dashboards, more deploy coordination.


Fourth, debugging. Every “why is this request slow?” investigation now crosses service boundaries.


Fifth, local/dev ergonomics. You either run more processes or fake the topology.


Sixth, architecture pressure. Once you have the internal services host, teams start shoving everything through it because it exists, even when in-process would be better.


That is the math.


What do you gain?


isolation

independent scaling

clearer network boundaries

potential reuse across multiple callers


Those are real gains. They just are not free.

When a domain gets its own host, it should also get its own non-overlapping boundary ownership as much as possible.


Otherwise you end up with:


central API routes wrapping domain-host routes

duplicated contracts

unclear ownership

extra hops for no reason


The clean mental model is:


services/* define capability boundaries
hosts own runtime placement
APIs are partitioned by domain ownership when a domain graduates


So yes:
add dedicated API/domain hosts when needed,
and split the central API tree accordingly so each host owns a clear surface.


That is much better than inventing a permanent all-services internal host up front.
what is a cli host? isnt my machine that?

Yes. Usually your machine is the CLI host.


“CLI host” is just the runtime role for command-based execution. It is not necessarily a server and not necessarily a deployed cloud host.


So in the same way that:


api host means “the process/environment running the API entrypoint”

async host means “the process/environment running async execution”


cli host means:


“the process/environment running the CLI entrypoint”


On your laptop, that is just your machine running bun run rawr ... or whatever your CLI command is.


In CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery), the CLI host could be:


a GitHub Actions runner

a Railway job

a container task

any ephemeral machine/process running the CLI entrypoint


So “host” here does not mean “server on the internet.”
It means “the environment/process role that mounts and runs that runtime surface.”


That is why the term still works.


Examples:


Local dev
  your MacBook
    -> CLI host for rawr commands
    -> maybe also web host if running frontend dev server
    -> maybe also api host if running local API


Railway prod
  service A -> API host
  service B -> async host


GitHub Action
  runner -> CLI host


So yes, your machine is the CLI host when you are running CLI plugins/commands.


The reason it is still useful to name it is semantic consistency. It tells you:


where CLI plugins mount

what runtime context they expect

how they differ from API/async/web/agent runtime surfaces


The main thing not to do is over-literalize “host” into “always a separate deployed machine.” In your model, host really means:


runtime entrypoint + execution environment


That can be:


one process on your laptop

one Railway service

one CI runner

one container

one VM


So the clean answer is:


Yes, your machine is the CLI host when running the CLI.
No, it does not imply some extra server or cloud box.
It is just the CLI runtime role.

Now the deeper part: does this support “just any part of the tree” being split out?


Yes, if the composition model is selector-based.


Meaning rawr.hq.ts should not be a giant handwritten monolith. It should be able to compose from declared plugin/service manifests by capability selectors or package lists.


For example:


createHq({
  includeCapabilities: ["sales", "billing"],
  includeRoles: ["server", "async"],
})


or more explicitly:


createHq({
  plugins: [
    salesServerApiPlugin,
    salesAsyncWorkflowPlugin,
    billingAsyncWorkflowPlugin,
  ],
  services: [
    salesService,
    billingService,
  ],
})


Then:


apps/shared/rawr.hq.ts includes everything

apps/sales/rawr.hq.ts includes just sales subtree contributions

apps/todo/rawr.hq.ts includes just todo contributions


That is how you get:


n=1

partial split

full split


without changing the core architecture.


So the answer to your question is:


Yes, the model supports n=1, but only if you explicitly represent shared assembly as a first-class app, not just domain apps.


The simplest statement of the final model is:


apps/roles/*      reusable role runtimes
apps/shared/*     n=1 whole-system assembly
apps/<domain>/*   split assemblies for promoted subtrees


That is the one to lock.


The sanity check is:


Can the same capability live:


in the shared assembly today

in its own domain assembly tomorrow

without changing its core service/plugin definitions?


If yes, the model is correct.


That is the goal.

host-bundles/shared/* composes some or all of those into one deployable bundle

This also means your host-first plugin split is even more defensible, because plugins are not pretending to be capability roots. They are explicitly runtime adapters over service-owned capability logic.


So your mental model should now be:


A service is not “something that must run remotely.”
It is the canonical capability unit.


A plugin is how that service gets projected into a specific runtime surface.


A host bundle is how a selected set of plugins/services gets assembled into a deployable system.


A role is one process/runtime role inside that bundle.


That also clarifies the transport question:


If services/todo defines the capability, then:


the server plugin can call it in-process if mounted locally

the async plugin can call it in-process if mounted locally

either can call it over RPC only if that service boundary is mounted remotely somewhere else


So services being the capability center does not force remote service hosts. It just means the service boundary is the canonical shape.
